summary: Overall UK risk remains Elevated. The dominant drivers are external: ongoing Middle East conflict dynamics continue to create risk to global energy supply chains and maritime security, with the Bank of England explicitly citing war-driven energy price rises as a near-term inflation and growth risk. Domestically, the most material near-term disruption signal is the ongoing six-day resident doctors’ strike in England, which raises short-term strain risk for NHS operations. Natural hazards remain broadly contained, with a warm spell peaking then cooler conditions expected, and the Environment Agency’s national flood forecast indicating very low risk despite a small number of active flood alerts.

categories.name: Economic / Financial
categories.rationale: Energy-price sensitivity remains the primary macro risk and is explicitly highlighted by the Bank of England; inflation remains above target on the latest ONS release, while UK market/FX indicators in reference sources show no acute dislocation.
drivers: Bank Rate held at 3.75% with BoE noting Middle East war-driven energy price rises and higher near-term inflation risk
ONS February 2026 CPI 3.0% y/y and CPIH 3.2% y/y; next CPI release scheduled 22 April 2026
Reference quotes: Brent around 104 USD/bbl; GBP/USD around 1.324; FTSE 100 around 9,697

categories.name: Military / Security
categories.rationale: Geopolitical tensions remain a material UK risk driver through energy and shipping exposure, with official UK messaging condemning attacks on commercial vessels/infrastructure and warning about shipping interference; UK domestic terrorism baseline remains at SUBSTANTIAL.
drivers: MI5 national terrorism threat level remains SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely)
GOV.UK joint statement cites attacks on commercial vessels and oil/gas infrastructure and warns of interference with shipping and disruption to global energy supply chains
Maritime advisories continue to flag elevated risk to UK-associated shipping in the Red Sea / Gulf of Aden region

categories.name: Political / Governance
categories.rationale: Governance remains stable, but the government has escalated domestic economic coordination via COBR for Middle East-related impacts and is considering targeted, time-limited regulator powers if price gouging emerges.
drivers: COBR(M) meeting focused on domestic economic impacts of the Middle East situation
Government exploring anti-profiteering framework and potential time-limited, targeted powers for CMA/regulators
No clear evidence in reviewed sources of acute UK constitutional or governance disruption

categories.name: Natural Hazards
categories.rationale: National flood risk is described as very low and active EA flood alerts are fewer than the prior update; a warm spell peaks then cooler conditions are expected, with no specific active Met Office warning listings visible in the reviewed page content.
drivers: Environment Agency flood forecast for the next 5 days indicates very low risk across England and shows 7 flood alerts
Met Office national forecast highlights very warm conditions peaking (up to ~26°C) then a return to cooler conditions
No specific active Met Office warning listings visible in the reviewed UK warnings page content

categories.name: Social Stability
categories.rationale: Ongoing resident doctors’ strike action in England remains the clearest near-term disruption driver, with NHS advising patients to attend appointments unless contacted and to use 111/999/A&E as needed.
drivers: Resident doctors’ six-day strike in England runs from 07:00 BST Tuesday to 07:00 Monday 13 April
NHS guidance: do not delay necessary care; attend appointments unless told otherwise; urgent and emergency pathways remain available
Potential knock-on impacts to elective care and local service resilience during sustained industrial action

categories.name: Critical Infrastructure / Cyber
categories.rationale: NCSC-linked reporting indicates active exploitation risk for a high-severity enterprise edge product vulnerability, reinforcing the near-term need for rapid patching and compromise checks across exposed estates.
drivers: NCSC urging immediate action on actively exploited F5 BIG-IP APM vulnerability CVE-2025-53521 (RCE) per reporting
Guidance emphasises patching, investigating for compromise, and hardening before reintroducing affected systems
Persistent background DDoS/ransomware threat environment for UK organisations

indicators.name: Brent crude
indicators.note: Trading Economics reference shows Brent at 104.15 USD/bbl (31 March), down 3.02% day-on-day and up 33.97% over the prior month on that page.

indicators.name: GBP/USD
indicators.note: Xe mid-market rate displayed 1.00 GBP = 1.32382092 USD (timestamp shown: 14:10 UTC on the page).

indicators.name: FTSE 100 (UKX)
indicators.note: TradingView reference displayed FTSE 100 Index at 9,696.74 GBP, up 0.44% over the past 24 hours on that page.

indicators.name: UK inflation (CPI/CPIH)
indicators.note: ONS CPI bulletin (Feb 2026) reports CPI 3.0% and CPIH 3.2% y/y; next release scheduled 22 April 2026.

indicators.name: Flood status (England)
indicators.note: Environment Agency national flooding service shows 7 flood alerts and states the flood forecast risk for the next 5 days is very low.

indicators.name: UK terrorism threat level
indicators.note: MI5 states the UK national threat level from all forms of terrorism is SUBSTANTIAL (attack is likely).

changes_since_last_update: Natural Hazards trend marked improving and flood indicator updated because Environment Agency now shows 7 flood alerts with a very low national forecast (previous update cited higher alert counts).

Critical Infrastructure / Cyber trend marked worsening due to NCSC-linked reporting of active exploitation of CVE-2025-53521 affecting F5 BIG-IP APM and emphasis on immediate patching and compromise checks.
Indicators refreshed from current reference pages (Brent, GBP/USD, FTSE 100) while overall status held at Elevated.
priority_watch_items: Middle East and maritime security: monitor any further attacks or constraints affecting Strait of Hormuz / Red Sea shipping and knock-on energy price impacts.
NHS disruption (through 13 April): monitor operational impacts of the resident doctors’ strike and any local critical-incident declarations.
Enterprise edge patching: prioritise rapid mitigation for CVE-2025-53521 (F5 BIG-IP APM) and confirm compromise-check procedures on exposed systems.
Inflation and policy path: watch for evidence of energy-price pass-through ahead of the ONS inflation release on 22 April.
Flooding: monitor whether flood alerts escalate to warnings, particularly groundwater and coastal hotspots.
source_notes: Market indicators are taken from publicly visible reference quote pages (Trading Economics, Xe, TradingView) which may not fully reflect live intraday pricing.
Inflation figures use the ONS February 2026 CPI bulletin PDF; the next release date is taken from the same document.
Flood counts and forecast are taken from the Environment Agency national flooding service page.
Cyber assessment is based on reporting summarising NCSC guidance on an actively exploited vulnerability; direct NCSC advisories may contain additional technical detail.
Met Office warning status is based on the content visible on the UK warnings page at the time of reading; warning listings may be more fully expressed via interactive elements or regional pages.
